nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and seaward reef slopes with rich coral growth, typically at depths of 0-60 m.
nativeRange.biotope: Reef-associated, non-migratory; East Africa to the Tuamotu Islands, north to the Izu Islands, south to Lord Howe Island. Absent from the Red Sea, Hawaii, and the southern Pacific.

diet.notes: In the wild it grazes primarily on algae and detritus, but in captivity it generally accepts a varied diet of spirulina, marine algae, high-quality angelfish preparations, mysis shrimp, and other meaty frozen foods. Small frequent feedings tend to be advised.

avoidWith: other dwarf angelfishes in small tanks

summary: The Coral Beauty Angelfish is one of the most popular dwarf angelfish in the saltwater hobby, valued for its deep blue flanks and orange iridescence. Generally considered one of the hardier marine angels, it has a mixed reputation for reef compatibility and has been known to nip at corals.
careNotes: The Coral Beauty Angelfish is among the more readily kept dwarf angelfish and is commonly recommended for aquarists making their first attempt at keeping a Centropyge. It tends to do best in a mature tank with plenty of live rock for grazing and multiple caves or overhangs to retreat into, as it is somewhat shy when first introduced. Reef compatibility is mixed: many individuals leave corals alone, but the species has been documented nipping at large-polyp stony (LPS) corals, soft corals, and clam mantles, so it is generally classed as reef-safe with caution. Like other Centropyge, it is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite — meaning females can transition to male — and naturally forms small harems of one dominant male with several females. In captivity it is most often kept singly; keeping a harem requires very large tanks (roughly 900 L / 240 US gal or more) started with juveniles of similar size. Conspecific and same-genus aggression is generally high, so only one dwarf angelfish per typical reef tank is advised.
breedingNotes: Pelagic spawning of small eggs at dusk has been observed in the wild, but the species is not commonly bred in home aquaria; rearing the planktonic larvae through metamorphosis is generally considered beyond hobbyist setups.

additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, distribution, max length (11.5 cm TL), preferred temperature (24.6–28.8°C, mean 27.5°C), and harem-forming social structure (3–7 individuals).

additional.name: Saltwater Aquarium World
additional.notes: Cross-checked pH (8.0–8.4), specific gravity (1.025), captive lifespan (8–10 yr, up to ~15 in the wild), care level (beginner), and reef-safe-with-caution rating.

fish.breedingNotes: The Coral Beauty Angelfish is rarely bred in captivity. While pelagic eggs and planktonic larvae have been described from wild spawning observations, rearing the larvae through metamorphosis is generally considered impractical in hobbyist setups.
